annotations.label: Euler's theorem

anchor.snippet: if n and a are coprime positive integers

annotations.note: `Nat.ModEq.pow_totient` states exactly `x ^ φ n ≡ 1 [MOD n]` under `Nat.Coprime x n`, with `ZMod.pow_totient` as an equivalent units-form statement.

annotations.label: Converse of Euler's theorem

anchor.snippet: The converse of Euler's theorem is also true

annotations.note: The converse direction `a ^ φ(n) ≡ 1 [MOD n] → Coprime a n` is not stated as a named lemma; the underlying infrastructure `ZMod.isUnit_iff_coprime` makes it a one-step corollary but no exact decl exists.

annotations.label: Ones digit of a large power of 7

anchor.snippet: consider finding the ones place decimal digit

annotations.note: This numerical worked example (computing 7^222 mod 10) has no corresponding decl in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Reducing exponents modulo the totient
annotations.note: `Nat.pow_totient_mod` states `x ^ k % n = x ^ (k % φ n) % n` whenever `1 < n` and `x.Coprime n`, exactly the exponent-reduction principle.

annotations.label: Group-theoretic proof via Lagrange's theorem

anchor.snippet: Euler's theorem can be proven using concepts from the theory of groups

annotations.note: Mathlib's proof of `ZMod.pow_totient` follows this exact group-theoretic argument via `pow_card_eq_one` on the unit group `(ZMod n)ˣ`, with `card_units_eq_totient` identifying its order as `φ n`.

annotations.label: Direct proof via reduced residue system

anchor.snippet: There is also a direct proof

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes Euler's theorem only via the group-theoretic route through `(ZMod n)ˣ`; the elementary product-permutation proof on reduced residue systems is not present.
